Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after work and my other commitments, but with 12 courses to juggle, I could never really stick to a plan. I'd make these detailed schedules on Sunday nights, feeling hopeful, but by Tuesday everything would fall apart. I ended up cramming whenever I could find a spare moment, which wasn't often enough.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening in my life that week. I'm 28 with a lot of responsibilities outside of school, so regular study times felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Some weeks I'd be up until 2 AM trying to catch up, other weeks I barely touched anything because I was dealing with family stuff or work. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied, not any kind of healthy routine.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd remember an assignment was due or realize I hadn't checked in for days and feel that familiar wave of anxiety. Sometimes I logged in just to see how far behind I was, which usually made me feel worse. Occasionally I'd get an email reminder about something and that would push me to check the LMS.
Q4: I really tried to make it a habit early in the semester, setting phone reminders and everything. For maybe the first two weeks, I was logging in daily and feeling good about it. But then the overwhelm hit when assignments started piling up across all my courses, and the routine completely collapsed. After that, logging in felt more like facing a mountain of failure than a normal part of my day.
Q5: The recorded lectures were honestly a lifesaver for me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while commuting or doing chores, which helped me feel like I was at least absorbing something. The discussion boards were also helpful when people actually responded to questions because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. I just wish I had more time to engage with both of these resources properly.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs because I don't have reliable internet at home and downloading everything was such a hassle. The practice quizzes I kept telling myself I'd get to 'later' but later never came. I also ignored a lot of the optional video content because I was so focused on just trying to complete the mandatory stuff that anything extra felt impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to an assignment or exam. If I could listen to something and feel like I actually understood a concept better, that was valuable. But honestly, with my anxiety levels so high, I often couldn't tell what was worth my time until it was too late. I kept second-guessing myself about what to prioritize.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a cruel joke sometimes. Like, I knew they'd probably help me learn better, but when I could barely keep up with required work, how was I supposed to find time for extras? Required activities at least had clear deadlines that forced me to engage, even if poorly. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing it.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I recorded myself summarizing lecture content and listened to those recordings constantly since I learn better through audio. I borrowed textbooks from the library and studied from those at home since I don't have internet. I also formed a small study group with two other students and we'd meet at a coffee shop to discuss material, which helped me process things verbally.
Q10: So many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS would show nothing. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times over the week while walking or cooking. My study group sessions were incredibly valuable but completely invisible online. I also spent hours talking through concepts with my sister who's in a similar field, and that verbal processing really helped me understand things.
Q11: All the time, actually. Given my internet situation, I'd go to campus or the library specifically to download everything I could, then work with those materials offline for days. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, then review those notes repeatedly at home. The LMS probably thought I only engaged with things briefly when really I was spending hours with the downloaded content.
Q12: Around week six, everything fell apart for me. My anxiety was through the roof, I was behind in almost every course, and I started having trouble sleeping. The more behind I got, the harder it was to even look at the LMS because it just reminded me of all my failures. I had a CBT session that helped my mood eventually, but by then I'd already missed so much.
Q13: Oh, absolutely. The workload across 12 courses was genuinely unmanageable, and I kept getting confused about which assignments belonged to which course. I was exhausted all the time from trying to balance school with everything else in my life. When I was that tired and overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen, unable to actually process anything or make decisions about what to do first.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my brain was just static. My anxiety would be so high that I couldn't actually absorb anything I was looking at. Sometimes I'd watch an entire lecture video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained a single thing because I was too busy worrying about everything else I needed to do.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly, especially the ones that felt too big or confusing. When I'm anxious, I freeze instead of act, so I'd tell myself I'd start 'tomorrow when I felt better.' The bigger and more important something was, the more terrifying it felt to start, so I'd do smaller, less important tasks instead. It's a terrible cycle and I know it's self-sabotaging but I couldn't seem to stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd ignore work until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then panic-submit whatever I could throw together. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because I was too overwhelmed to even attempt the work. The deadline pressure actually made my anxiety worse, which made it harder to focus, which made me more behind. It was awful.
Q17: I honestly didn't get much feedback because I wasn't submitting enough work to receive it. When I did get feedback, it was often too late to really help because I was already drowning in the next thing. The few times I got encouraging comments, it did motivate me briefly, but that motivation would fade quickly under the weight of everything else I was dealing with.
Q18: The course structure felt really rigid and didn't accommodate someone like me who was juggling so much. Everything had strict deadlines with no flexibility, which meant one bad week could snowball into disaster. I needed more scaffolding and check-ins, maybe smaller chunks of work spread out differently. The structure assumed I had consistent time and energy to give, which I just didn't have.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement really hard. There was so much material that I never felt like I could get on top of it. Having 12 courses at once meant the LMS was overwhelming to even navigate. What would have helped is clearer prioritization of what was truly essential versus nice-to-have, and maybe some flexibility in deadlines for students with complicated lives.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in repeatedly, click through things, watch videos, read discussion posts, but then when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze. The anxiety of producing something that would be judged was so much harder than passively consuming content. Also, sometimes you're genuinely trying to understand material but the understanding doesn't translate into completed assignments.
Q21: I can see how that happens, especially for people who study effectively offline like I tried to do. If someone has good resources outside the LMS and studies efficiently, they might not need to log in much but still learn the material. For me, I did a lot of learning offline but my exam performance didn't reflect it because my test anxiety was so severe that I couldn't demonstrate what I actually knew.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you clicked on things, which gives a surface picture of engagement. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, whether you were actually absorbing anything or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, group work, verbal processing, and all the mental and emotional barriers that affect how someone interacts with material. They're measuring activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It means being mentally present when you're studying, asking questions, making connections to things you already know. For me, engagement also means being able to manage my anxiety enough to actually participate rather than just surviving. True engagement requires mental bandwidth that I often didn't have this semester.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite my poor results. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to recordings offline, the study group sessions, the tears of frustration. It wouldn't capture my genuine desire to succeed or how much my anxiety and overwhelm sabotaged my efforts. It also wouldn't show that after my CBT session, I finally started feeling more hopeful, even though my grades couldn't recover by then.
